quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Center of mass (discrete)"?

options.text: F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum
A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force
x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total; the mass-weighted average position of a collection of particles
The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout

quiz_questions.explanation: "Center of mass (discrete)" is defined as: x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total; the mass-weighted average position of a collection of particles. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Center of mass (continuous)"?

options.text: When τ_net,ext = 0: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂; if I decreases, ω increases proportionally
τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)
x_cm = (1/M)∫x dm; requires integration for objects with continuous mass distributions
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Center of mass (continuous)" is defined as: x_cm = (1/M)∫x dm; requires integration for objects with continuous mass distributions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Center of mass motion"?

options.text: F_c = mv²/r = mrω²; the net radial force required to maintain circular motion at a given radius and speed
Oscillation where F = -kx and x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); characterized by constant period independent of amplitude
F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Center of mass motion" is defined as: F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Velocity of center of mass"?

options.text: KE_rot = ½Iω²; energy due to rotation, analogous to ½mv² for translation
P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second
v_cm = Σm_i v_i / M_total = p_total / M_total; constant for an isolated system
U = -GMm/r; defined as zero at infinite separation; always negative for bound systems

quiz_questions.explanation: "Velocity of center of mass" is defined as: v_cm = Σm_i v_i / M_total = p_total / M_total; constant for an isolated system. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Center of mass reference frame"?

options.text: A frame moving with v_cm; simplifies collision analysis because total momentum is zero in this frame
ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration
F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it
In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision

quiz_questions.explanation: "Center of mass reference frame" is defined as: A frame moving with v_cm; simplifies collision analysis because total momentum is zero in this frame. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total; the mass-weighted average position of a collection of particles"?

options.text: Angular frequency
Weight
Center of mass (discrete)
Hooke's Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Center of mass (discrete)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "x_cm = (1/M)∫x dm; requires integration for objects with continuous mass distributions"?

options.text: Recoil
Tangential velocity
Center of mass (continuous)
Newton's First Law

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Center of mass (continuous)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there"?

options.text: Energy in pendulum
Simple pendulum period
Center of mass motion
Instantaneous power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Center of mass motion".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "v_cm = Σm_i v_i / M_total = p_total / M_total; constant for an isolated system"?

options.text: Gravitational PE (universal)
Velocity of center of mass
Angular impulse
Escape condition

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Velocity of center of mass".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A frame moving with v_cm; simplifies collision analysis because total momentum is zero in this frame"?

options.text: Center of mass reference frame
Torque
Gravitational field
Dot product

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Center of mass reference frame".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Center of Mass is accurate?

options.text: Universal Gravitation states that every mass attracts every other mass with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg².
Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
Gravitational Potential Energy in the universal form is U = -GMm/r, where r is the distance between centers of mass.
Center of Mass is the mass-weighted average position of a system: x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total (or r_cm = ∫r dm / M for continuous distributions).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Center of Mass as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Center of Mass?

options.text: A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.
The center of mass moves as if all the system's mass were concentrated there with F_ext = Ma_cm.
AP Physics C emphasizes setting up and evaluating these integrals.
AP Physics C emphasizes calculus-based kinematics, using derivatives and integrals to describe motion with non-constant acceleration.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Center of Mass as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Center of Mass?

options.text: Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.
Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
For an isolated system, the center of mass velocity is constant regardless of internal interactions, making it invaluable for analyzing explosions and multi-body systems.
Kinematics: Motion in One Dimension describes the motion of objects along a straight line using position x(t), velocity v(t) = dx/dt, and acceleration a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Center of Mass as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Center of Mass?

options.text: Friction problems often involve inclined planes, circular motion, and connected bodies.
Center of Mass is the mass-weighted average position of a system: x_cm = Σm_i x_i / M_total (or r_cm = ∫r dm / M for continuous distributions).
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
Dynamics problems in AP Physics C require setting up and solving differential equations when forces depend on velocity or position.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Center of Mass as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Center of Mass?

options.text: Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.
The center of mass moves as if all the system's mass were concentrated there with F_ext = Ma_cm.
Conservative Forces and Potential Energy connect force to potential energy through F = -dU/dr (in one dimension) or F = -∇U (in three dimensions).
This inverse-square law governs planetary orbits, satellite motion, and tidal effects.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Center of Mass as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Center of Mass?

options.text: Spacecraft docking maneuvers calculating the center of mass of the combined vehicle to plan thruster firings for orbit adjustments
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Calculating the energy required for rocket launches: the difference in gravitational PE between Earth's surface and the desired orbital altitude plus the kinetic energy needed
Analyzing bungee jump physics using conservation of energy between gravitational PE and elastic PE to determine maximum stretch and maximum speed

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Center of Mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Center of Mass?

options.text: Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Center of Mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Center of Mass applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Analyzing braking distance on inclined roads where both gravity component and friction determine deceleration
Earthquake-resistant building design using tuned mass dampers (large SHM systems) to counteract the building's oscillation and reduce structural damage
Automotive engineering placing a vehicle's center of mass low and centrally to optimize handling, stability, and rollover resistance
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Center of Mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Center of Mass?

options.text: Spacecraft docking maneuvers calculating the center of mass of the combined vehicle to plan thruster firings for orbit adjustments
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Structural engineering resolving forces on bridge cables into horizontal and vertical components to analyze tension and compression in each member

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Center of Mass.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Center of Mass in practice?

options.text: High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Structural engineering calculating forces on bridge components using free-body diagrams and Newton's laws to ensure safe load-bearing capacity
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Center of Mass.
